perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that the Confidential Information will be kept confidential and that Recipient and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that (i) it may make any disclosure of the Confidential Information to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any of the Confidential Information may be disclosed to Recipient’s Representatives who need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction, who are bound by confidentiality obligations to Recipient or agree to keep such Confidential Information confidential to the same extent as Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that the Confidential Information will be kept confidential and that Recipient and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that it may make any disclosure of the Confidential Information to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that allows disclosure to the Recipient's Representatives if they are bound by confidentiality agreements, the contract now places undue restrictions on information sharing necessary for evaluating a potential transaction. This could hinder compliance with securities laws, as it limits the ability to consult with legal and financial advisors.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 requires companies and individuals to avoid making false statements or omissions of material fact that could mislead investors. Impeding the flow of information to advisors can hinder due diligence and increase the risk of non-compliance with this rule, especially in the context of evaluating a possible transaction. The change prevents effective legal consultation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the allowance for disclosing confidential information to the Recipient's Representatives who are bound by confidentiality obligations, which was present in the original text. The Law Explanation states that this impedes the flow of information to advisors, potentially leading to non-compliance with Rule 10b-5. Snippet 1 states Rule 10b-5 makes it unlawful to 'omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made... not misleading.' The Perturbation Explanation also states that this change hinders compliance with securities laws, as it limits the ability to consult with legal and financial advisors. By limiting the flow of information to advisors, the changed text increases the risk of misleading statements or omissions, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Recipient or any of its Representatives are requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information, Recipient shall provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice of any such request or requirement so that the Disclosing Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Recipient or any of its Representatives are requested or required to disclose any of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information, Recipient may disclose the information as required.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for prompt written notice to the Disclosing Party, the Recipient could disclose confidential information without allowing the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order. This could violate trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets. By removing the notice requirement, the Recipient is able to disclose information more readily without the disclosing party being able to protect their trade secrets by legal avenues.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Recipient to provide prompt written notice to the Disclosing Party if confidential information is requested or required to be disclosed. This contradicts the purpose of trade secret law, as explained. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses civil actions related to trade secret misappropriation, stating that 'an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection'. By removing the notice requirement, the Disclosing Party is prevented from seeking a protective order, impacting their ability to prevent misappropriation. This omission directly undermines the protections intended by trade secret laws. The change in the contract removes an essential component for protecting trade secrets, as the Disclosing Party's ability to take legal action is severely hindered without prior notice.

perturbation.original_text: Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, upon the request (which request shall be made within a reasonable time after such termination of discussions) of the Disclosing Party for any reason, Recipient will, at Recipient’s option, either deliver to the Disclosing Party or destroy (which destruction shall be certified in writing by an officer of Recipient) all Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party pursuant hereto and the portion of all other Confidential Information prepared by Recipient or its Representatives which contains Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party; provided, however, that Recipient and its Representatives (i) may each retain one copy of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for recordkeeping purposes and for the purposes of defending its rights and obligations hereunder and (ii) will not be required to return or destroy -2- any computer or other electronic hardware or systems, to render any electronic data irrecoverable or to disable or otherwise modify any existing electronic data backup procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, Recipient will retain all Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the option for the Disclosing Party to request the return or destruction of confidential information after termination of discussions could conflict with data protection regulations if the information contains personally identifiable information (PII) and the retention period is not justified. This is also in conflict with the idea of good faith negotiation if the deal falls through.
perturbation.law_explanation: Chapter 93A prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in business. Retaining confidential information indefinitely after the termination of discussions, without any reasonable business justification or consideration for the disclosing party's interests, could be deemed an unfair or deceptive practice, especially if the information contains sensitive data or trade secrets. It removes the opportunity for the other party to request return or destruction of the data after the deal has fallen through.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the Disclosing Party's right to request the return or destruction of confidential information. The Law Explanation indicates that retaining confidential information indefinitely after termination could be considered an unfair or deceptive practice under Chapter 93A, especially if it contains sensitive data. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides general information about Chapter 93A, it doesn't detail specific requirements for handling confidential information post-termination. However, the removal of the option for the Disclosing Party to request data destruction directly contradicts the principle of fair business practices which is the premise of the law. The omission creates a situation where the Recipient could potentially misuse or improperly retain sensitive information, which the original clause aimed to prevent, supporting the presence of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: It is further understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including an injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach by the Recipient of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: It is understood and agreed that money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that money damages are a sufficient remedy and removing the right to equitable relief (injunction, specific performance), the contract limits the Disclosing Party's recourse in case of a breach. This might be problematic because money damages may not adequately compensate for the loss of trade secrets or other confidential information, especially given that the contract is based in Massachusetts.
perturbation.law_explanation: MUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By limiting the remedy to money damages, the agreement weakens the protection of trade secrets afforded by Massachusetts law, as monetary compensation may not always be adequate to address the irreparable harm caused by the loss of confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Disclosing Party's right to equitable relief, specifically injunctive relief, by stating that money damages are a sufficient remedy. The Law Explanation states that MUTSA (Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act) allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 42, defines misappropriation, which injunctive relief is designed to prevent. By limiting the remedy to money damages, the modified contract directly weakens the protection afforded by Massachusetts law, as monetary compensation might not adequately address the irreparable harm caused by the loss of confidential information or a trade secret. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the law by limiting the available remedies, which creates a legal contradiction.
